mappings.rationaleAI: Furniture as movable functional objects supporting human activities places it squarely in interior decoration objects (C.3.c). Its historical traditions from Egyptian joinery through Modernism, overlap with industrial design and interior design, and its role as both functional and aesthetic object situate it within the broader nature of decorative arts (A) and interior design as an art (C.3).

Pottery as the shaping and firing of clay bodies into earthenware, stoneware, or porcelain is a direct match for decorative objects classified by clay materials (B.1). Its dual nature as functional vessel and ceramic art connects to the nature and scope of decorative arts (A).

Jewellery made from precious metals, gemstones, glass, shell, and enamel connects to decorative objects using gems (B.3). As decorative worn items — rings, necklaces, bracelets — it directly addresses jewelry as dress design and body decoration (C.1.b).

The intentional creation of plans for objects balancing aesthetic and functional goals within constraints encompasses the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A). Its coverage of product design and constraint-driven purposive creation directly addresses industrial design as an art (C.2.a) and industrial design broadly (C.2).

Items worn on the body for environmental protection, social status signaling, and fashion directly address dress design (C.1.a) and the broader category of dress design and body decoration (C.1). Coverage of dress accessories, modesty norms, and cultural dimensions of clothing reinforces the connection.

Flowers as reproductive structures of angiosperms connect to the decorative use of flowers, foliage, and related botanical materials such as bouquets, garlands, and wreaths (B.10.a). Though primarily a botanical article, its relevance to floral decoration and arrangement is genuine.

Textiles as fibre-based materials produced by weaving, knitting, crocheting, and felting directly address decorative objects classified by fabrics (B.2). As one of the oldest industries connecting fashion, cultural history, and material culture, textiles relate to the nature and scope of decorative arts (A).
Glass as an amorphous silicate solid used in vessels, windows, and decorative objects since Mesopotamia directly addresses decorative objects classified by glass materials (B.4).
Paper as thin sheets of matted cellulose fibers directly addresses decorative objects classified by paper materials (B.6), which includes papier-mache and wallpaper.
Bronze as a copper-tin alloy widely used in sculpture, coinage, architectural elements, and decorative mechanical objects directly addresses decorative objects classified by metals (B.5).

Gold as a noble metal used in jewelry, gold-leaf art, and coinage addresses decorative objects classified by metals (B.5). Its extensive use in rings, necklaces, and decorative items connects to jewelry (C.1.b).
Copper as a ductile transition metal used in architecture, coinage, and decorative patinas addresses decorative objects classified by metals (B.5).

Metallurgy as the science of metallic elements, alloys, and their properties underpins the production and decoration of metal decorative objects (B.5), covering ferrous and non-ferrous metals used in decorative and functional design.

Bone as a mineralized connective tissue connects to the decorative use of shell, horn, bone, ivory, and related materials (B.10.d). Bone has been carved and shaped for decorative objects throughout human history.

Shoes as paired footwear items for protection and comfort, differentiated into athletic, dress, dance, and fashion categories, directly address dress and dress accessories (C.1.a) and the broader category of dress design and body decoration (C.1).

Visual arts as static art forms including ceramics, design, and crafts, with the fine arts vs. applied/decorative arts distinction discussed since the Arts and Crafts Movement, provides broad context for the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A).

Masonry as construction from brick, stone, concrete block bonded with mortar directly addresses decorative objects classified by stone (B.7). Its use of plaster, cement, and concrete connects to special inorganic materials (B.9.a).

Natural rubber as an elastomeric biopolymer connects to plastics and other synthetic materials (B.9.b) in the context of decorative and functional materials, though it is a natural rather than synthetic polymer.

Iron as the basis of steel and wrought-iron decorative work directly addresses decorative objects classified by metals (B.5). Iron has extensive use in architectural ornament, railings, and functional design objects.

Architecture as the design and construction of habitable structures, with emphasis on interior design and the integration of decorative elements, connects to interior design as an art (C.3.a) and the integration of interior design with architecture including ceilings, floors, walls, and windows (C.3.b).

Sculpture as three-dimensional art produced by carving stone, casting metal, modeling clay, and assembling materials connects to decorative objects in stone (B.7) and metals (B.5). Its overlap with decorative arts and material traditions situates it within the nature and scope of decoration (A).
Toys as objects used for entertainment and play — dolls, construction sets, vehicles, puzzles — directly address the design and decoration of play materials (C.5.b).

Painting as the application of pigment to surfaces including walls and glass connects broadly to the nature of decorative arts (A). Its mention of encaustic and various media connects to painted enamels within enamelwork (B.11.a).

Alphabets as writing systems with symbols representing phonemes connect to handwriting systems and styles, calligraphy, and lettering (C.4.a), as the design of letterforms is foundational to calligraphic and typographic arts.
Arabic script as the basis of Arabic calligraphy, written in cursive form right-to-left, directly addresses calligraphy and handwriting systems and styles (C.4.a).

Vehicles as machines for transport encompassing land, water, air, and space modes directly address the design of transportation equipment as a kind of industrial design classified by function (C.2.b).
Drawing as visual art practice encompassing commercial illustration, animation, and architectural drafting connects to illustration within the printing arts and printing design (C.4.b).
Hokusai as a ukiyo-e artist known for woodblock prints and book illustrations connects to printmaking and illustration within the printing arts (C.4.b).
